title: Bucida correlliana
intro: Bucida correlliana, also known as the tropical bucidia tree, is a plant species in the family Combretaceae that is native to the tropical regions of Central America and the Caribbean. The leaves and bark of Bucida correlliana have been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin infections, due to their antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antiseptic properties. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In traditional herbal medicine, Bucida correlliana is commonly prepared as a tea, decoction, or infused oil, which can be used to treat a range of health issues, including respiratory infections, wounds, and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Bucida correlliana is classified as a species within the family Meliaceae, which is a family of flowering plants commonly known as the mahogany family. This family consists of approximately 600 species, including both evergreen and deciduous trees and shrubs. The genus Bucida itself is native to the Americas, particularly in Central and South America, as well as the Caribbean. Bucida correlliana is a member of this genus and is native to the Americas as well. It is often referred to as the "Mexican bucidia" due to its distribution in Mexico and other parts of the continent.
